## SECTION 8 — This Day in History (World)
On September 5, 1972, the incredibly massive, utterly horrific, and absolute darkest day in Olympic history aggressively and violently unfolded during the Munich Summer Games. Heavily armed Palestinian terrorists belonging to the Black September faction aggressively breached the incredibly lax Olympic Village security, brutally killing two Israeli athletes and heavily taking nine others hostage. The massive, deeply botched rescue attempt by German police resulted in the incredibly tragic, horrific deaths of all the hostages, permanently altering the massive security architecture of global sporting events.

On September 5, 1905, the incredibly massive, highly destructive, and fiercely fought Russo-Japanese War officially and aggressively concluded. Brokered heavily by US President Theodore Roosevelt, the massive Treaty of Portsmouth was formally signed. The treaty heavily recognized Japan's absolute, monumental victory, aggressively ceding control of Korea and the massive Liaodong Peninsula to the ascendant Asian empire. This massive defeat absolutely humiliated the Russian Empire, fiercely catalyzing the massive domestic unrest that sparked the 1905 Russian Revolution.

## SECTION 9 — This Day in History (India)
On September 5, 1888, Dr. Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan, the incredibly massive, universally revered Indian philosopher, towering intellectual, and statesman, was officially born in Thiruttani. An incredibly brilliant scholar who deeply defended Hinduism against uninformed Western criticism, he heavily served as the very first Vice President and the absolute, highly distinguished second President of the independent Republic of India. His massive, profound contributions to education and philosophy are heavily honored annually across India on his birthday, which is celebrated as National Teachers' Day.

On September 5, 1997, Mother Teresa, the incredibly massive, universally beloved, and absolutely iconic Catholic nun and missionary, peacefully passed away in Calcutta. She had heavily dedicated her entire massive life to incredibly aggressive, selfless service, fiercely tending to the deeply impoverished, sick, and dying in the massive slums of India. Her incredible, massive humanitarian work fiercely earned her the Nobel Peace Prize and her death triggered an absolute, unprecedented global outpouring of massive, profound mourning and saintly devotion.
